text: The surgeon accesses the abdomen at the umbilicus using the Hassan technique, placing a mini gel port and insufflating the abdomen. A laparoscopic camera is inserted, revealing the appendix with visible signs of acute inflammation.

The surgeon grasps the appendix along its mesentery and elevates it, displaying the mesoappendix prominently. Electrocautery is used to divide the mesoappendix, ensuring hemostasis by rocking the hook up and down. The proximal appendix is cleared of residual fatty mesentery. Two PDS endo-loop sutures are used to divide the appendix; the first endo-loop is positioned over the appendix, cinched down at its base, and the first suture is cut. The cecum, appendix, bloodless fold of Treves, and terminal ileum are clearly visible. A second endo-loop is placed distal to the first suture.

The surgeon transects the appendix using scissors, leaving a cuff of appendiceal tissue to prevent suture slippage. The appendix is then extracted through the umbilical port site. The appendiceal base is visually examined, showing effective occlusion and hemostasis.

The surgeon visualizes the right and left fallopian tubes and ovaries, both appearing normal with no free fluid in the pelvis. The left and right upper quadrants are inspected, showing the liver, stomach, and gallbladder as normal, with no reactive fluid or pus present in the abdominal region.

The surgeon removes the gel port and reduces the pneumoperitoneum. The fascia is closed with two O-vicryl sutures, and local anesthetic is infiltrated at the incision site. The skin is closed with monocryl suture, and a cotton ball with a small Tegaderm dressing is applied.
